articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Police Recover Ancient Egyptian Artifacts the Day After a Heist at a Museum in Australia
The looted items included a 2,600-year-old wooden cat figurine, a 3,300-year-old necklace and a mummy...
articles.content:
Police Recover Ancient Egyptian Artifacts the Day After a Heist at a Museum in Australia
The looted items included a 2,600-year-old wooden cat figurine, a 3,300-year-old necklace and a mummy mask
Police have recovered a trove of ancient Egyptian artifacts stolen from a museum north of Brisbane, Australia, after a short investigation and manhunt.
The day after the artifacts vanished from the Abbey Museum of Art and Archaeology, officials arrested 52-year-old Miguel Simon Mungarrieta Monsalve. “The defendant believed it was his duty to acquire the artifacts and return them to their rightful place,” prosecutors explained in court earlier this week.
Magistrate Deborah Vasta described the heist and Monsalve’s alleged intentions as “bizarre,” comparing the incident to “something from a ‘Simpsons’ episode,” according to Lottie Twyford of the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC).
Authorities apprehended Monsalve on Russell Island in Queensland. Police allege he was in possession of a 2,600-year-old wooden cat figure from Egypt’s 26th Dynasty. The rest of the stolen goods—including a 3,300-year-old necklace, a mummy mask and an ancient Egyptian collar—were discovered in Monsalve’s camper van, parked at a ferry terminal south of the museum, according to officials.
Quick fact: The jewels stolen from the Louvre
The theft in Australia appears to have been less calculated than the high-profile heist that occurred at the Louvre in October, when thieves entered the museum through a second-floor balcony and made off with French crown jewels, estimated to be worth over $100 million, in mere minutes.
At roughly 3 a.m. on February 13, a man smashed a museum window, entered and took several Egyptian artifacts, according to a statement from the Queensland Police. The stolen objects are thought to be worth more than $100,000.
However, if the artifacts are severely damaged, “they have no value regardless,” Nina Pye, the museum’s manager, told the ABC’s Joshua McIntosh before the items were found. She encouraged the thief to return the trove to the museum, “where it can benefit the community and education as a whole.”
The items recovered from the van sustained only minor damage, though they will need to be professionally assessed before they are put back on display. Other objects suffered more serious harm. Several ushabtis—small figurines that were placed in tombs and believed to be servants and manual laborers for the deceased in the afterlife—were damaged beyond repair during the heist, according to Agence France-Presse.
“To find somebody has taken [the artifacts] using violence … smashed the glass to take them without any care or thought or any consideration for their fragility or their age—they’re all in temperature-controlled displays—to expose them to the Queensland weather at the moment is just a horrendous thing,” Michael Strong, a curator at the museum, tells the ABC.
The stolen items were reportedly often used as part of the museum’s educational programs, which allow students to participate in hands-on digs and study artifacts from the ancient world.
According to prosecutors, Monsalve said that he believed the museum had ties to the Catholic Church, which was part of his motivation for stealing the items. He was charged with breaking and entering and three counts of willful damage, and he is being held without bail.

articles.title: Couples Have Been Kissing Under the Lovers' Arch in Italy for Years. On Valentine's Day, It Collapsed Into the Sea
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Couples Have Been Kissing Under the Lovers’ Arch in Italy for Years. On Valentine’s Day, It Collapsed Into the Sea
The iconic rock formation crumbled after days of raging storms. Local officials are...
articles.content:
Couples Have Been Kissing Under the Lovers’ Arch in Italy for Years. On Valentine’s Day, It Collapsed Into the Sea
The iconic rock formation crumbled after days of raging storms. Local officials are calling for new initiatives to help slow coastal erosion in the region
A large rock formation in Italy known as “Lovers’ Arch” is now a pile of rubble. Located near the town of Melendugno, Puglia, the landmark was beloved by photographers and tourists alike. It collapsed during a storm on Valentine’s Day.
“It is a devastating blow to the heart,” Maurizio Cisternino, Melendugno’s mayor, tells the local newspaper Corriere Salentino’s Gaetano Gorgoni, per a translation by the Guardian’s Lorenzo Tondo. “One of the most famous tourist features of our coastline and of the whole of Italy has disappeared.”
The arch on the coast of Salento was one of the many rock formations that make up the Faraglioni di Sant’Andrea. Over hundreds of years, wind and waves molded the limestone into its distinctive shape. But as Weather.com’s Jenn Jordan writes, “The same forces that created the arch ultimately destroyed it.”
Quick fact: What is Salento?
Commonly described as the heel of Italy’s boot, Salento is a peninsula in the country’s southernmost region situated between the Adriatic and Ionian Seas.
In the late 18th century, the arch acquired a romantic reputation. Local legend held that couples who kissed or swam together beneath the rocks would be rewarded with lasting love, reports CNN’s Barbie Latza Nadeau. More recently, it’s attracted thousands of visitors, who sought out the arch to take photos, propose marriage and celebrate romantic milestones.
Fortunately, the rock formation caved in on a winter night when nobody was kissing, taking photos or swimming beneath it. If it had collapsed in July or August, when tourists typically flock to the site, the results could have been tragic, Giovanni Caputo, president of the Order of Geologists of Puglia, tells Fanpage.it’s Maria Neve Iervolino.
Recently, storms have raged through much of the region, battering the coastline with surges of heavy rain, according to Corriere Salentino. These harsh conditions devastated beach resorts, damaged ports and caused sections of cliffs to crumble. According to Reuters, severe weather has generated more than $1.2 billion worth of damage in southern Italy this year.
When Lovers’ Arch collapsed, storms had been wearing away at it for days. “We have lost one of our region’s defining characteristics, a symbolic asset,” said Antonio Decaro, Puglia’s regional president, to reporters at the site on February 16, per BBC News’ Kerena Cobbina. “Unfortunately, natural processes are often accelerated by meteorological phenomena.”
Caputo tells Fanpage.it that 50 percent of Puglia’s coastline is eroding. He adds that this erosion is worsened by severe weather, and that the collapse of Lovers’ Arch could have been more effectively monitored and slowed, even if it couldn’t have been prevented.
“With the Mediterranean [experiencing] among its hottest years on record in 2025, warmer seas are supercharging the atmosphere and fuelling extreme events,” Christian Mulder, an ecologist at the University of Catania in Sicily, tells the Guardian.
The region needs to initiate interventions to combat coastal erosion, Cisternino tells Corriere Salentino. But while Melendugno submitted plans for a roughly $5.3 million project, the effort hasn’t moved forward due to a lack of funding.
“Nature has been overturned. What existed 30 years ago no longer exists,” Cisternino adds, per a translation by the Guardian. “We must find the resources for an organic intervention.”

articles.title: Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born 'Inside Out'
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born ‘Inside Out’
A rocky planet was found orbiting its host star in an unusual location
Planetary systems...
articles.content:
Astronomers Discover an Exoplanet in the Wrong Place, Hinting at a Planetary System That Was Born ‘Inside Out’
A rocky planet was found orbiting its host star in an unusual location
Planetary systems in the Milky Way galaxy tend to follow a particular pattern: rocky planets toward the center, closest to the host star, and gas planets toward the exterior. That’s how planets are organized around our sun, with rocky Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars first in the lineup, followed by the gaseous Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune.
But astronomers have found a rule breaker. The cool and faint red dwarf star LHS 1903, some 116 light-years away, hosts four planets that clearly don’t care for convention, since a rocky planet somehow seems to orbit at the outer edge, boxing in two gas planets and another rocky planet that lies closest to the star. The findings, published February 12 in the journal Science, might hint at an unusual mechanism behind the birth of some planets.
“[The LHS 1903 system] follows that [standard] pattern beautifully for the first three planets,” study co-author Andrew Cameron, an astronomer at the University of St. Andrews in Scotland, tells Science News’ Adam Mann. “Then, something weird happened to the fourth planet.”
Computer simulations typically indicate that a planetary system’s innermost members are rocky because the host star’s energy blasts away their atmospheres. Gas giants, on the other hand, tend to take shape at a greater distance, in colder areas where gas can build up.
Cameron and his colleagues found the outlier while looking at data collected from 2019 to 2023 by NASA’s Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), which searches distant stars for signs of orbiting planets. That revealed a planetary system around the star LHS 190, which is roughly half the sun’s mass. Follow-up observations with other telescopes unveiled some properties of the four planets, including size, mass and density, which hinted at their compositions and unusual order.
So, what’s up with the rocky weirdo in the wrong place? After ruling out some possible explanations, computer simulations hint that the outermost planet, called LHS 1903 e, may have been born without an atmosphere. The planetary system may have followed an “inside-out” formation mechanism, in which the inner planets came together before the outer ones—the opposite way that the solar system’s planets are thought to have emerged.
“[That] means the outermost planet formed millions of years after the innermost one. And because [LHS 1903 e] formed later, there was actually not that much gas and dust in the disk left to build this planet from,” study co-author Thomas Wilson, a planetary astrophysicist at the University of Warwick in England, tells CNN’s Jacopo Prisco.
The out-of-place planet could be an astronomical weirdo, or it may offer a first glimpse at a previously unobserved planetary system dynamic, per a statement.
Fun fact: How many exoplanets have we found?
Last year, NASA’s official tally reached 6,000 exoplanets. Still, more than 8,000 candidates were awaiting confirmation at that time.
“The team that conducted this work consists of experts at the top of the field, and they have done a very fine job with the data,” says Lauren Weiss, an astrophysicist at the University of Notre Dame who wasn’t involved in the new study, to Scientific American’s Jacek Krywko. “As for their conclusion that LHS 1903 e formed in a gas-depleted environment, I would have liked to see a more detailed experiment exploring the giant-impact scenario,” she adds. The study authors doubt that the rocky planet started with gas and lost it via some dramatic collision.
Néstor Espinoza, an astronomer at the Space Telescope Science Institute who was not involved in the work, tells CNN that the discovery will help researchers better understand the birth of planets, especially around tiny stars like LHS 1903. “This system adds a very interesting data point that will have planet formation models trying to explain it for years to come—and I’m sure we will learn something new about the process of planet formation once they are compared against each other,” he writes in an email.

articles.title: Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
A new study found that young baboons try to interrupt their mother when she is grooming a sibling
If you have siblings, you may remember fighting...
articles.content:
Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
A new study found that young baboons try to interrupt their mother when she is grooming a sibling
If you have siblings, you may remember fighting for your parents’ attention when you were younger. It turns out sibling rivalry isn’t just something that affects humans. A new study has found that young baboons try to steal their mothers away from their brothers or sisters.
Scientists observed baboon families in Namibia between August and December 2021, and noticed that when a mother was grooming a child, a sibling—often an older one—would try to interrupt them. The baboon vying for parental attention would bite, slap, cry and sometimes even lure their sibling away in order to supplant their place in their mother’s arms.
Because baboons usually have one baby every 18 to 24 months, “it was generally thought there was no real competition between siblings, because brothers and sisters are different ages and do not necessarily need their mother and her resources at the same time,” explains Axelle Delaunay, a behavioral ecologist at the University of Turku in Finland and the study’s first author, to Agence France-Presse.
However, sibling interference in baboons “strikingly mirrors patterns of sibling jealousy reported in humans,” Delaunay and her colleagues write in their study, published in the journal Proceedings of the Royal Society B on February 11.
“It’s totally relatable,” says Joan Silk, a primatologist at Arizona State University who was not involved in the work, to Annie Roth at the New York Times. “I have two sisters and two children, so I can relate, but the fact that it’s relatable doesn’t always mean it’s true.”
In this case, it likely is. “This group of researchers has really pushed the envelope about what we know about relationships between individuals,” Silk tells the Times.
Overall, Delaunay and her team found that baboons are more likely to interfere when their mother is grooming a sibling than when she is resting and already free. Offspring were also twice as likely to interfere against their younger siblings, and targeted siblings of the same sex more often. Interference was most common between brothers, they found.
Female baboons also have “favorite” children that they groom disproportionately more than others. The researchers found that the baboons target that favorite child, lending more evidence towards the idea that the interrupting behavior is motivated by jealousy. “Our findings further indicate that baboons are aware of their own maternal bond relative to those of their siblings,” the authors write in the study, though further studies are needed to determine the cognitive complexity of these skills.
And good news for the mothers: the jealous behavior faded away as the baboon got older.
Delaunay and her team aren’t sure what benefits these displays of sibling rivalry have for the jealous offspring. While sibling interference interrupted bouts of grooming in 19 percent of instances, it was only successful in getting the interrupter access to grooming by their mother in 9 percent of cases. “Most of the time, interference doesn’t really work,” Delaunay tells the Times. “So what are the benefits?”
She hopes that future research will answer that question, and that more scientists will study primate emotions. “While we can’t ask them how they feel, we know that emotions provoke physiological changes, behavioral changes, some cognitive changes—and this you can measure,” she adds.
